attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about yellow discolouration of her skin that has developed gradually over three months. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She follows a strict vegetarian diet and started juicing papaya and sweet potatoes daily six months ago. She denies any abdominal pain or nausea, but notes consistently loose bowels with no haematochezia. Despite her relatively healthy diet, she drinks 5 beers per night for the past 10 years and smokes 15 cigarettes per day.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is diffuse yellow-orange skin pigmentation on her palms and soles. Her sclera are white. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with characteristic features of carotenoderma, including yellow-orange skin discolouration that is most prominent on the palms and soles, with sparing of the sclera. The history of excessive consumption of carotenoid-rich foods (such as carrots, papaya, peach, beans, broccoli, pumpkin, cucumber, and sweet potatoes) strongly supports the diagnosis. The loose bowels are likely secondary to the excessive juice consumed.

Carotenoderma is a benign condition usually caused by excessive dietary intake of carotenoids or, less commonly, reduced carotenoid metabolism (through diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism or hepatic disease) leading to excessive deposits in the stratum corneum. It occurs with carotenaemia which describes when the blood level of β-carotene is above the normal limit. It does not cause any toxicity and only can be associated with amenorrhea which may be due to the existing malnutrition rather than carotenoderma.

Jaundice would typically involve yellowing of the sclera, which is absent in this case. Though her alcohol intake is excessive and she has loose bowel motions, the presentation is more fitting to carotenoderma. Additionally, jaundice would be accompanied by other symptoms of liver dysfunction such as dark urine, pale stools, or abdominal pain.

Addison disease causes hyperpigmentation that is more brown than yellow-orange and is particularly prominent in sun-exposed areas and areas of friction. It would also be accompanied by fatigue, postural hypotension, and salt craving.

Haemochromatosis can cause skin bronzing but typically presents with other manifestations of iron overload such as diabetes, arthropathy, or liver disease. The bronzing tends to be more grey-brown than yellow-orange.

Quinacrine toxicity can cause yellow skin discolouration but requires exposure to the medication, which is not present in this patient's history. It also tends to cause a more intense yellow colour and can affect the sclera.

Management of carotenoderma involves dietary modification with reduction of carotenoid-rich foods. The condition is entirely benign and will resolve spontaneously once dietary intake is normalised. No specific treatment is required.
learningPoints: Carotenoderma presents with yellow-orange skin discolouration, particularly prominent on the palms and soles.
Excessive intake of carotenoid-rich foods is the most common cause of carotenoderma.
Unlike jaundice, carotenoderma spares the sclera and mucous membranes.
references: Ngan V, Schukow P, Keefe M, Mitchell G. Carotenoderma. DermNet NZ. 2021.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/carotenoderma
Edigin E, Asemota IR, Olisa E, Nwaichi C. Carotenemia: A Case Report. Cureus. 2019.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6758952/
